QENNA WRITER
About Source Releases License

Qenna Writer

free and open software for writing novels and screenplays

A desktop app for drafting, organizing, and finishing long fiction — chapters, characters, worldbuilding, and revision tools in one place. Built in C++ and Qt, so it opens in under a second and stays out of your way.

Look first Read the source Windows · ~120 MB Download Qenna Writer

v0.8.0 · Windows · Qenna Writer License · no account, no subscription

NOTE —

About the Windows warning: the installer isn't code-signed — that costs money this project doesn't spend — so SmartScreen will show "Windows protected your PC" on first run. Click More info, then Run anyway. Normal for small indie tools.

I — The editorQenna Writer

Writing software that gets out of your way

Focus Mode, inline comments, cross-document tagging with @, and proper screenplay formatting (Scene, Action, Character, Dialogue, Parenthetical, Transition) when your project needs it — without an AI rewriting your sentences for you.

the editor — Chapter 1

II — Keeping it organizedQenna Writer

Every story, organized your way

Drawers for characters, settings, objects, and lore. Multiple manuscripts in a single project — draft a whole trilogy without losing track of book one. The Reference panel opens beside the page, so a chapter three books ago is one click away, not a lost tab.

  • Unlimited documents, folders, and cross-references
  • Reference Menu — jump to any chapter or scene instantly
  • Search across the current document, the project, or any drawer
  • Focus Mode dims everything but the paragraph you're on — works fine with the Reference panel open beside it

Focus Mode, with the Reference panel open

III — Pin it before you write itQenna Writer

A board for the parts that aren't prose yet

Post-its, character cards, images, and linked docs on a single canvas, connected however the plot actually connects. Not a mind-map template with fixed nodes — a blank board you fill however this story needs.

  • Post-its, images, character cards, and doc links, all on one board
  • Freeform connections between any two cards
  • Colored zones to group arcs, factions, or plotlines
  • Send any card straight to the Timeline as an event

the Planning Board

IV — Never lose the threadQenna Writer

Know who's where, and who said what

Automatic character-presence detection across chapters and scenes, a relationship graph built from how often characters actually appear together, and a searchable dialogue log — every line, sorted by who said it.

V — Time keeps itselfQenna Writer

A timeline the story builds on its own

Give a chapter a date and a one-line summary — two fields already sitting there, easy to ignore — and it becomes an event on its own. From a single start date, chapters before it fall into Flashback, chapters after it stay in the main line. When the story genuinely forks — a different cast shows up, time jumps backward, a new POV takes over — a branch appears without being asked, and folds back into place the moment the lines meet again.

  • Built entirely from what you're already writing — a chapter's date and summary, nothing extra to maintain
  • Confirms a branch by a repeated pattern, not by one out-of-place chapter, so it doesn't overreact
  • Every event already knows who's in it; a character filter dims everything but one thread
  • A Timeline Generator backfills an old project's chapters in one sitting, instead of one at a time
VI — Before you write a lineQenna Writer

Plan the world before you draft the sentence

The Builder designs magic systems, religions, and political structures as real trade-offs — every choice on the spectrum lists what it favors and what it demands, not just flavor text. The World Map keeps geography honest — real countries, a ruler for real distance, and pins that stay put wherever your story happens.

VII — Keeping the habitQenna Writer

The tools that keep you writing after the spark fades

Word or time-based goals, streaks, Pomodoro sprints — and a calendar that remembers every session, so a bad week is visible instead of vague, not just a vague feeling.

VIII — Making it yoursQenna Writer

143 themes, or build one from scratch

Page color and panel color are independent — a dark interface can wrap a bright white page, or the other way around. Set a day theme and a night theme and let Qenna Writer switch on a schedule. With this many, a search box and a favorites list got added this week, because scrolling stopped being reasonable around theme 80.

a slice of the theme grid

What's actually being written in itQenna Writer

A few real drafts, mid-progress

Not renders, not stock covers — these are open manuscripts from writers using Qenna Writer right now, taken straight from a live Library screen.

IndexQenna Writer

Everything else, briefly

  • ExportDOCX, ODT, PDF, and EPUB — whole project, one manuscript, or a single chapter.
  • Screenplay modeReal screenplay elements with correct margins and casing, not just centered text pretending to be one.
  • Ambient soundSoundscapes and focus music, built in — no browser tab full of rain sounds.
  • Cover CreatorA separate free tool bundled alongside it, for designing a cover from royalty-free art.
  • RemindersFlag anything left loose so a chapter gets revisited before it goes cold.
  • Three languagesPortuguese (Brazil), English, and Spanish, switchable in Settings.
Source & licenseQenna Writer

License

The Qenna Writer License — based on GPLv3. Anyone can read, change, and redistribute this code for free, but nobody can fork it into a closed, or a paid, product.

One person builds and maintains this in spare time. Bugs get filed on GitHub Issues and are usually fixed in days, not quarters — there's no support queue to wait in.

Links

Source code — Pedrovskigg/qenna-writer Build from source Releases & installers Third-party notices Cover Creator (separate repo)

Contact

To report bugs, suggest new features, or just scream about your plot at 3:00 AM: qennawriter@gmail.com.

I can take a few hours or days to answer, but I will — I promise. Or reach me on GitHub, on Qenna's repository.

"You shouldn't have to pay to write good stories, or pay for the tools that help you write them. The only payment Qenna Writer asks for is: write good stories."
— P.H. Lobato, creator of Qenna Writer
